expect: Within S350+ era, session concurrency (N active lanes) positively predicts per-lesson citation out-degree (r>=0.10). Falsified if r<0.10 or negative.

concurrency_effect.measure: same-day session count from git log

session_recency_effect.note: No time trend within S350+ era — citation conventions stabilized

external_content_effect.note: Having external references does NOT predict higher citation density

frontier_tag_effect.note: Frontier assignment has zero effect on citation density

diff: PRIMARY HYPOTHESIS FALSIFIED: concurrency r=0.04 (ns for Cites: header, barely p<0.05 for body refs). Below r=0.10 threshold. The STRONGEST predictor of citation density within S350+ is word count (r=0.24, p<0.001), followed by domain (eta^2=0.121, F=3.18, p<0.001). Concurrency, session recency, frontier tags, and external references are all non-significant or trivially small. Domain explains 12.1% of variance — a 3.7x range from helper-swarm (1.33) to security (5.00). The S508 Simpson's paradox is now explained: what looked like a session-type effect was really (1) an era effect (citation conventions), and within-era (2) a domain+length effect, not a coordination or session-type effect.
verdict_detail: Session concurrency does not predict citation density (r=0.04, ns). Lesson word count (r=0.24) and domain assignment (eta^2=0.12) are the only meaningful within-era predictors. Longer lessons cite more because they have more text in which to embed references. Domain differences reflect domain-specific citing norms (security/NK lessons cite heavily; helper-swarm/governance cite sparsely). The original S367 finding (DOMEX>HARVEST) and S372 regression (DOMEX_pct drives K_avg) remain true as era markers, but the causal mechanism is citation convention adoption, not session type or concurrency.
falsification_criteria: r<0.10 for concurrency vs cites → TRIGGERED
self_apply: Measuring what drives citation density changes the citation network itself — this lesson will cite several L-NNN references, feeding the hub attractor. The finding that domain predicts density means NK-complexity's own measurement practice is self-reinforcing (high-citing domain measures citation → confirms high citing).
